title: Dice Score / F1-Score
concept: The Dice score (equivalent to F1-score) measures mask overlap, giving slightly higher values than IoU and penalizing near-misses differently.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does IoU ignore True Negatives?

options.text: To prevent large background areas from inflating the performance score
To make the calculation faster on old CPUs
To allow the model to run without validation labels

exercises.explain: Correct! Leaving out True Negatives keeps the metric focused on object overlap.

cards.title: The twin metric
cards.body: While computer scientists love IoU, medical researchers almost always report the Dice Score (also known as the F1-Score). They measure the same thing: overlap. But they use slightly different math, which changes how they penalize mistakes. Let's see how they compare.

cards.title: Dice Score / F1-Score
cards.body: The Dice Score is defined as: $$Dice = \frac{2 TP}{2 TP + FP + FN}$$. It is mathematically identical to the F1-Score. It counts True Positives twice in both the numerator and denominator, yielding higher values than IoU for the same overlap.

cards.title: A slightly kinder grader
cards.body: If IoU is a strict grader who gives you a C (60%) for a minor boundary error, the Dice score is a kinder teacher who gives you a B (75%) for the same paper. Both agree you made a mistake, but Dice doesn't penalize it as harshly.

cards.title: Dice vs. IoU values
cards.body:
If a model's prediction overlap has a TP of 80, FP of 10, and FN of 10:
- $IoU = 80 / (80+10+10) = 0.8$
- $Dice = (2 \times 80) / (2 \times 80 + 10 + 10) = 160 / 180 = 0.89$.
The Dice score is higher than IoU.

cards.title: They are monotonic
cards.body: You might think a model could have a high IoU but a low Dice score. That's a myth! They are monotonic: if Model A's Dice score is higher than Model B's, its IoU will also be higher. You can convert between them using: $IoU = Dice / (2 - Dice)$.

left.text:
TP / (TP + FP + FN)
Penalizes mistakes harder
E.g., 0.70 score

right.text:
2TP / (2TP + FP + FN)
More widely used in medical papers
E.g., 0.82 score

exercises.prompt: What is the mathematical relationship between the Dice Score and F1-Score?

options.text: Dice is the square of F1
F1 is double the Dice score

exercises.explain: Correct! Dice Score and F1-Score are two names for the exact same formula.

exercises.prompt: True or False: For any imperfect overlap, the Dice score is mathematically higher than the IoU score.
exercises.explain: True. Because TP is weighted twice, Dice values are always slightly higher than IoU (except at 0 and 1).

exercises.sentence: The Dice score is mathematically identical to the ___ score used in classification.
exercises.explain: The Dice score is the exact same formula as the F1-score (harmonic mean of precision and recall).

exercises.prompt: If a model has an IoU of 0.5, what is its Dice score?
A medical paper claims their U-Net achieves an F1-score of 0.85 on tumor masks. Another paper claims an IoU of 0.75. Which model has better overlap?

options.text: The second model (IoU 0.75), because an IoU of 0.75 converts to a Dice/F1 score of approximately 0.86, outperforming the first model.
The first model (F1 0.85), because 0.85 is larger than 0.75.
Neither, because F1-score and IoU cannot be compared.
The first model, because F1-score is only used when the model is 100% accurate.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Since $Dice = 2 \times IoU / (1 + IoU)$, an IoU of 0.75 yields a Dice score of $1.5 / 1.75 \approx 0.857$ (85.7%). Thus, the 0.75 IoU model is slightly better than the 0.85 F1 model. Next: let's look at confusion matrices!
